ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general crab food product class; no specific BBQ combo crab dish class exists. Mapped to closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crustaceans broadly; no BBQ-specific crab dish code exists. Adapted from cooked crustacean category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for BBQ combo crab dish identified; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.description: 2 XL crabs, typically shared by 2–4 people
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of this dish is two extra-large whole crabs weighing approximately 1,200 g in total, typically shared between two to four diners as part of a seafood meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) adapted with BBQ marinade adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked blue crab (FDC ID 175180) as the closest approved-source equivalent for whole cooked crab. Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to account for BBQ marinade (soy sauce, garlic, ginger, chilli) consistent with typical Chinese-Singaporean BBQ crab preparation. Input record values (energy 150 kcal, protein 15 g, fat 8.33 g, carbohydrate 2.5 g, sodium 291.67 mg per 100 g) are broadly consistent with USDA cooked crab baseline plus marinade contribution and are retained as the best available estimate. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a specific BBQ combo crab entry; USDA FDC used as secondary global source per source priority policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate for very low carbohydrate seafood dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: BBQ combo crab contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. No direct GI measurement exists for this dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. An analogous GI of 5 is assigned based on the established principle that protein- and fat-dominant seafood dishes with negligible carbohydrate content have a very low GI (typically 0–10), consistent with published GI values for other low-carbohydrate seafood preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 5 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 30 g carbohydrate in 1,200 g serving) / 100 = 1.5, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 291.67 mg per 100 g; a full 1,200 g shared serving delivers approximately 3,500 mg sodium total, which is significant even when divided among 2–4 diners
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request BBQ sauce on the side, limit chilli and soy-based condiments, and restrict their personal portion to no more than 300 g of crab meat to keep sodium intake within the 2,000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (15 g per 100 g) support glycaemic stability; BBQ marinade may contribute small amounts of added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: BBQ combo crab is a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; request sauce on the side to minimise added sugar from the marinade, and avoid pairing with high-GI staples such as white rice or white bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 83.33 mg per 100 g and sodium at 291.67 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat is relatively low at 2.08 g per 100 g and the seafood provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease may consume BBQ crab in moderate portions (150–200 g per person) as part of a heart-healthy diet, prioritising the lean crab meat over sauce, and limiting total weekly dietary cholesterol to remain within clinical guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Crab provides omega-3 fatty acids and selenium with anti-inflammatory properties; BBQ marinade may include nightshade ingredients (chilli, pepper) that can aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of crab but should request preparation without chilli or nightshade-based sauces if they are sensitive to solanine-containing ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Combo Crab XL (2 Pieces) is a Chinese-Singaporean seafood dish featuring two extra-large whole live crabs (生猛活螃蟹) prepared using a BBQ or grilling method with a savoury marinade typically composed of garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and chilli. The dish is a popular sharing platter in Singapore's Chinese seafood restaurants and hawker-style BBQ establishments, often ordered as a centrepiece for group dining. The crab flesh is tender, sweet, and slightly smoky from the grilling process, with the marinade imparting umami depth and mild heat. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 150 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 15 g per 100 g, moderate fat at 8.33 g per 100 g (of which saturated fat is low at 2.08 g), and very low carbohydrates at 2.5 g per 100 g, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood option. Sodium is borderline at 291.67 mg per 100 g, primarily from the soy-based marinade and BBQ sauce. Cholesterol is present at 83.33 mg per 100 g, consistent with crustacean seafood. The dish is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load, and for those following heart-healthy diets due to its lean protein and omega-3 fatty acid content. Sodium management is the primary caution for hypertensive individuals. The standard serving is two XL crabs totalling approximately 1,200 g, typically shared by two to four diners.
common_user_queries: How many calories in XL BBQ crab?
Can I eat BBQ crab if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein in BBQ crab?
Is BBQ crab high in cholesterol?
What is a combo crab set in Singapore?
How much sodium in BBQ crab?

confidence_reason: No exact BBQ combo crab dish entry exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC cooked blue crab (FDC ID 175180) with marinade adjustment, and are broadly consistent with the input record values sourced from GrabFood. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is defensible but not an exact match to a named approved-source entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Chinese-Singaporean BBQ combo crab preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. USDA FDC cooked blue crab entry (FDC ID 175180) was consulted but does not provide all micronutrient fields at the required specificity for a BBQ-marinated preparation. Glycemic index is assigned as 5 (low) using an analogous estimate for a very low carbohydrate seafood dish; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving is 1.5, calculated as (GI 5 × 30 g carbohydrate in 1,200 g serving) / 100. The dish is not certified halal as preparation method and sauce ingredients may include non-halal components; halal status should be confirmed with the specific restaurant. Trans fat reported at 0.04 g per 100 g from input record; this is a trace level consistent with natural trans fats present in crustacean seafood and is not from industrial hydrogenation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to closest_parent; verified aliases consistency between flat array and aliases_by_language.

Iteration 3: Replaced null glycemic_index with numeric value 5 (analogous estimate, low GI category) to satisfy q4_glycemic_completeness hard requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Updated gi_source with peer-reviewed journal reference and analogous estimate methodology. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving to 1.5. Updated notes to reflect GI analogous estimate rationale rather than not_applicable classification.
